hints: Each ant picks one of 2 endpoints: 2³=8 outcomes.
No collision only if all ants circulate the same way (all clockwise or all counter-clockwise).
solution: Each ant chooses one of two adjacent vertices ($2^3=8$ outcomes). No two share a corner only when all three move the same rotational direction — clockwise or counter-clockwise — which is $2$ outcomes. So $P=\tfrac28=\tfrac14.$

hints: Runs = 1 + (number of adjacent pairs with different colors).
Each of the 99 adjacent pairs differs with probability 2·50·50/(100·99)=50/99.
solution: The number of runs equals $1$ plus the number of adjacent color changes. Each of the $99$ adjacent pairs has different colors with probability $\dfrac{2\cdot50\cdot50}{100\cdot99}=\dfrac{50}{99}$, so by linearity $\mathbb{E}[\text{runs}]=1+99\cdot\tfrac{50}{99}=51.$

hints: Let A be his age: A(1/4+1/5+1/3) + 13 = A.
solution: With age $A$, $A(\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac13)+13=A$. Since $\tfrac14+\tfrac15+\tfrac13=\tfrac{47}{60}$, the remaining $\tfrac{13}{60}A=13$, so $A=60.$

hints: Try 1+r+r²+… for small ratios r>1 and at least 3 terms.
121 = 11², the smallest such square.
solution: Taking ratio $r=3$ with five terms, $1+3+9+27+81=121=11^2$, a perfect square. This is the smallest square achievable by such a progression, so the answer is $121.$

hints: Averaging 60 mph over 2 miles needs total time 2 minutes.
The first mile at 30 mph already takes 2 minutes.
No time remains for lap 2 ⇒ impossible ⇒ −1.
solution: An average of $60$ mph over $2$ miles requires a total time of $2$ minutes, but the first mile at $30$ mph already consumes $2$ minutes. No speed can complete the second lap in zero time, so the answer is $-1.$

hints: Monday = 2, so add 2.
2 + 2 = 4 (Wednesday).
solution: Since $100\equiv2\pmod7$, the day is $2$ past Monday: $2+2=4.$

hints: The coupon rate (6%) equals the market rate.
A bond priced at a yield equal to its coupon trades at par.
Price = par = 1000.
solution: With the coupon rate equal to the market yield ($6\%$), the bond trades at par, so the price equals the face value, $\$1000.$

hints: Turning a pair changes the head count by ±2 or 0, so its parity is fixed.
Even initial heads → reach 4; odd → reach 3.
solution: Flipping two coins changes the number of heads by an even amount, so its parity is invariant. From an even count you can reach $4$ heads, from an odd count $3$. The initial count is even or odd each with probability $\tfrac12$, so $\mathbb{E}=\tfrac12(4)+\tfrac12(3)=\tfrac72.$

hints: Model states as (barrel, 7-vessel, 5-vessel) and search for the shortest pour sequence.
Each fill, empty, or transfer between containers counts as one transaction.
The minimum is 17 transactions.
The adversary concentrates on whichever n minimizes pₙn², so you equalize pₙn² across n.
pₙ ∝ 1/n²; Σ1/n² = π²/6 forces pₙ = (6/π²)/n².
The guaranteed value is 6/π², so a=6,b=2,ab=12.
solution: The opponent will place all mass on the $n$ minimizing $p_n n^2$, so you maximize the minimum by making $p_n n^2$ constant: $p_n\propto\tfrac1{n^2}$. Normalizing, $p_n=\dfrac{6/\pi^2}{n^2}$ and the guaranteed payout is $p_n n^2=\dfrac{6}{\pi^2}$. Thus $a=6,b=2$ and $ab=12.$

hints: Bulb k is toggled once per divisor of k.
It ends on iff k has an odd number of divisors — i.e. k is a perfect square.
solution: Bulb $k$ is flipped once for each divisor of $k$, so it ends on iff $k$ has an odd number of divisors, which happens exactly for perfect squares. There are $10$ squares ($1^2,\dots,10^2$) up to $100$, so $10$ lights are on.

hints: Ignore all rolls that aren't a 5 or a 2.
Sum 5 has 4 ways, sum 2 has 1 way.
solution: Conditioning on a decisive roll (sum $5$ or $2$), sum $5$ has $4$ combinations and sum $2$ has $1$, so $P=\dfrac{4}{4+1}=\dfrac45.$
